original_abstract:
This paper introduces a comprehensive framework for sentence-based image description and search by conceptualizing the problem as a ranking task. Rather than focusing on the linguistic complexities of generating novel captions, the authors propose a retrieval-based approach where systems must associate images with descriptive sentences drawn from a predefined pool. To facilitate this, the authors present the Flickr 8K dataset—a collection of 8,000 images, each annotated with five high-quality, crowdsourced captions that focus on salient entities and events.

The study explores several models based on Kernel Canonical Correlation Analysis (KCCA) and nearest-neighbor search, utilizing low-level perceptual features and various text kernels. The experimental results demonstrate that incorporating multiple captions during training, alongside semantic and syntactic features such as word order and lexical similarities, significantly improves performance. Furthermore, the paper provides a rigorous evaluation of both human and automatic metrics. By leveraging large-scale crowdsourced relevance judgments, the authors show that ranking-based metrics (such as R-precision and recall at top-k) are more robust than traditional generation metrics like BLEU or ROUGE. Crucially, the analysis suggests that the rank of the original caption serves as a reliable proxy for human judgment, potentially allowing for the fully automated evaluation of image understanding systems.
humanized_abstract:
In this paper, we introduce a general framework for the task of sentence-based image description and search, under the name of Image Description and Retrieval (IDR). Instead of trying to resolve the language-exploration challenges inherent in generating a new description for each given image, we aim at reformulating the task as a ranking problem. To this end, we design a retrieval-based framework in which, for a given image, an image description generator needs to select a suitable sentence description from a pre-built sentence set. To support the proposed IDR task, we introduce a new image caption dataset known as the Flickr 8K dataset: it consists of 8,000 images with 5 well-explained, human-generated captions for each image. The captions are crowdsourced and describe, accordingly, mainly important objects and events.

This work explored a number of approaches centered around Kernel Canonical Correlation Analysis (KCCA) and nearest-neighbor search that make use of low-level visual features and text kernels. They explored multiple models that make use of multiple captions as training data, as well as a number of features including both semantic and syntactic (i.e., word order and lexical co-occurrence). The work evaluates both human and automated evaluation metrics. While the metrics used for image description evaluation such as BLEU and ROUGE are commonly compared to the human judgment they aim to model, the authors show through large-scale evaluation of crowdsourced relevant judgements of a subset of the dataset that ranking metrics such as R-precision and recall at rank k provide much more stable evaluation metrics than commonly used evaluation metrics for image description. They also show the rank of the original caption is a highly correlated measure of human judgements for an image description evaluation framework and so in principle can be evaluated without any human involvement.

document.output:
In this paper, we introduce a general framework for the task of sentence-based image description and search, under the name of Image Description and Retrieval (IDR). Instead of trying to resolve the language-exploration challenges inherent in generating a new description for each given image, we aim at reformulating the task as a ranking problem. To this end, we design a retrieval-based framework in which, for a given image, an image description generator needs to select a suitable sentence description from a pre-built sentence set. The captions are crowdsourced and describe, accordingly, mainly important objects and events.

This work explored a number of approaches centered around Kernel Canonical Correlation Analysis (KCCA) and nearest-neighbor search that make use of low-level visual features and text kernels. They explored multiple models that make use of multiple captions as training data, as well as a number of features including both semantic and syntactic (i.e., word order and lexical co-occurrence). The work evaluates both human and automated evaluation metrics. While the metrics used for image description evaluation such as BLEU and ROUGE are commonly compared to the human judgment they aim to model, the authors show through large-scale evaluation of crowdsourced relevant judgements of a subset of the dataset that ranking metrics such as R-precision and recall at rank k provide much more stable evaluation metrics than commonly used evaluation metrics for image description. They also show the rank of the original caption is a highly correlated measure of human judgements for an image description evaluation framework and so in principle can be evaluated without any human involvement.
document.input:
This paper introduces a comprehensive framework for sentence-based image description and search by conceptualizing the problem as a ranking task. Rather than focusing on the linguistic complexities of generating novel captions, the authors propose a retrieval-based approach where systems must associate images with descriptive sentences drawn from a predefined pool. To facilitate this, the authors present the Flickr 8K dataset—a collection of 8,000 images, each annotated with five high-quality, crowdsourced captions that focus on salient entities and events.

The study explores several models based on Kernel Canonical Correlation Analysis (KCCA) and nearest-neighbor search, utilizing low-level perceptual features and various text kernels. The experimental results demonstrate that incorporating multiple captions during training, alongside semantic and syntactic features such as word order and lexical similarities, significantly improves performance. By leveraging large-scale crowdsourced relevance judgments, the authors show that ranking-based metrics (such as R-precision and recall at top-k) are more robust than traditional generation metrics like BLEU or ROUGE. Crucially, the analysis suggests that the rank of the original caption serves as a reliable proxy for human judgment, potentially allowing for the fully automated evaluation of image understanding systems.
